title: 5 Questions to Ask Before You Sign Any Commercial Lease
excerpt: Most business owners sign commercial leases without legal advice, without proper comparison, and without asking the questions that would change the outcome. These five are the non-negotiables.
author: Joe Kelley
metaTitle: 5 Questions to Ask Before Signing a Commercial Lease | Your Office Space
metaDescription: Five questions every business owner should ask before signing a commercial lease — outgoings, make-good, rent review, permitted use, and exit rights.
body:
Commercial leases are binding, long-term contracts. Most run for 3 to 5 years minimum, with financial exposure that can run into the hundreds of thousands.

And yet most business owners sign them the same way they sign an iPhone terms-and-conditions update — quickly, under pressure, without reading the parts that matter.

These five questions change that.

## 1. What are the outgoings — and what's the cap?

Outgoings are the building's operating costs charged back to you on top of rent. Council rates, water, insurance, building management, lift maintenance, fire services — they add up.

In a net lease (the most common form in Australian commercial property), outgoings are charged to tenants separately. On a mid-grade building they typically add $80–$150/sqm per year.

Before you sign:
- Ask for the current year's estimated outgoings schedule
- Ask for two years of audited actuals
- Check whether there is a cap on outgoings increases
- Confirm which items are included and which aren't (land tax is a common dispute)

## 2. What does make-good actually require?

At the end of your lease, the make-good clause tells you what you have to do to the premises before you hand back the keys. In its worst form, it requires full reinstatement — you strip out everything you installed and return the space to base building condition.

For a fitted-out tenancy, that cost can be $50,000 to $200,000+.

Before you sign:
- Get the clause explained clearly by a commercial solicitor or tenant rep
- Push to include fair wear and tear exclusions
- Negotiate a cash settlement option at the landlord's election
- Document the condition of the space at the start of the lease with signed photos

## 3. How does rent review work?

You're not just agreeing to a starting rent — you're agreeing to a formula that will govern every increase over the life of the lease.

Fixed percentage reviews (3–4% annually) are the most predictable. CPI reviews track inflation. Market reviews reset rent to current market at a specified date — and in a rising market, they can spike significantly.

Before you sign:
- Model out total rent payments over the full lease term under each scenario
- If there's a market review, negotiate a ratchet clause (rent cannot fall below current level) AND a cap on uplift
- Understand which year the market review hits — year 3 of a 5-year lease is very different to year 5

## 4. Does the permitted use cover everything you actually do?

The permitted use clause defines what activities you are allowed to carry out in the premises. If you need to run client consultations, store stock, operate equipment, or expand your offering — and it's not in the permitted use — you may technically be in breach of your lease.

Before you sign:
- Read the permitted use clause carefully and list every activity your business carries out
- If anything is ambiguous, get written confirmation from the landlord that it's permitted
- Check that the permitted use aligns with the zoning certificate for the building

## 5. What are your exit rights?

Life changes. Businesses change. The lease you sign today needs to accommodate those changes.

Key exit mechanisms:
- **Assignment** — can you transfer the lease to a buyer if you sell the business?
- **Subletting** — can you sublet part or all of the space if you downsize?
- **Early termination** — is there a break clause? Under what conditions?
- **Renewal options** — do you have the right to renew? At what rent?

The strongest position going into a lease is knowing exactly how you can get out of it — and on what terms.

## The bottom line

These questions are not complicated. But they require time, attention, and the willingness to push back on a document the landlord's lawyer has spent years refining to favour the landlord.

If you're signing a commercial lease in the next 90 days and haven't had a tenant representative look at it — it's worth the conversation. Run the lease through our free LeaseIntel™ tool at yourofficespace.au/lease-review or book a call and we'll give you a straight read.
